speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is August the 5th, 1996. And we are conducting an interview with Rachel Drabkin. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] We were talking about the first news that you had from your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This-- this was all my-- I had a picture from my husband, a-- a whole group of them. And I couldn't keep them. So-- because everything they took away from me. So I took off just the little head from my husband, and a wedding ring. And this I was hiding under my tongue. And whenever I got-- going to sleep, when I was lying with my cousin, I took out the picture and said, look, this make me alive. I have to find my husband. He will be alive. And this keep me-- this little picture give me so much courage to go everything through, and hope this come a day when I-- when I find him. I was really lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you hear the news from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. There was somebody come, a man I think come from Estonia. They bring him something. Oh, he was professional, or they needed something there. They bring him to us in Strasdenhof. And he told-- he asked me, what's your name? And because I said to somebody I'm Drabkin. He said, oh, Drabkin? Drabkin, I heard from somebody. And this was the father with the five sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you celebrate any Jewish yontifs in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows when the yontif is? Who knows when yontif? Nobody knows how-- they know exactly the dates. We know it's cold in winter. And the sun is shining, that's all. If you see the sun-- the sun, but I don't think we ever saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did any major incidents really stand out in your memory of time at camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you what, it was in this Strasdenhof, there come one, a German. There was a kapo. He said, they're doctors, and he said to all of us, you have to dress completely naked. So everybody look at each other. What we going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So like I said to them, look. For me, it's like a dog would be more like them. What I care about it? But there was girls, there was beauties-- really beautiful, and all dressed. There was young, young children. They wasn't even married. So we all dressed up completely naked. And then you were standing between two sticks and look it, if you haven't got any-- any lice. And who he likes, he took on the side. This was a terrible experience time, terrible. This was once, and that's all what I can remember, something like that. Did the girls come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your feeling towards the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, towards the girls, I just feel sorry for them. Because I went through it the normal way, already in ghetto. But I've been through when I married, something, something which come with love. But for them, that was something where they have to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they talk about what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't think so, they talk about it, no. We know. We know what they're going to do anyway. It was a-- it was a shame even to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Strasdenhof, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Strasdenhof, we went to Stutthof. And there was a very, very big camp. I can't remember if this was exactly by Danzig, or it was somewhere closer to-- that was in Poland. And there, I wouldn't stay too long, maybe a month. I-- I'm not so sure exactly. And from there, they took out all the healthy womans who want to go work. The Germans come and said, I needed five, ten womans to work. So I went for work there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so once I went there, there come one from the field. And he said, look, I need five womans to work in a field. And they were saying, I was trying together with my cousin. And I was lucky. He come up. He took me. He took my cousin and another three womans to the field. And when we come there, it was very rich, very rich. This was not Poland, this [GERMAN] that was living like on a path which belong on-- where they speak only German, you know? And then when I come there, they asked me, the-- the lady what she-- she had the the house there, she ask me if I can sew on a machine. I said, yes. She said to me, can you make a bra? I said, yes. She said, how can you make it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you give me paper. And I cut a form. Well, you give me material, and I will sew it. So I had a holiday. So I didn't go work on the field. I was at house. They give me food. They give me everything. They didn't know what to do with me. I sewed them a bra, I sewed them a skirt. I don't know. I sew everything. I make it first on a paper. And then I sew it from the material what they give it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So this a very short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you stay in the house with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: With them, yes. Yeah, all of them stay in the house with them, all five girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were the only one that did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I did the work at home, by sewing machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they was working on the field, but they had it very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get from Strasdenhof to Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- I think we went by-- I can't remember exactly. But I think we went by buses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you Stutthof? A short time?

speaker: interviewee
text: A short time, because Stutthof was really a-- a camp-- what-- they send the people to work. And I was-- if I heard of somebody who come to German and said, you want to work? I went out, straight away with my cousin. I can work. So they send us to-- to dig-- what do you call-- the-- the holes for the protect to the soldiers. And it was winter. And there was no air. It was like goo. It were so hard. And we didn't have any shoes. So we covered the feet with [NON-ENGLISH] around-- around the feet. And we used to work there, and we're passing the field that go to work, they used to beetroot-- wild beetroot used to grow there. So the girls was running, and took them, eat them. Just clean them up like that, and eat them. Because we was hungry. We didn't get nothing. This was healthy. There was sugar. There was everything. There was vitamin in them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you just ate them raw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Raw, raw, yeah. It was-- lucky to get that or a potato raw. That was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Stutthof, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Stutthof, I went-- I went to Kolkau, where we're supposed-- because there we was digging the [NON-ENGLISH], what you call for-- for the soldier. And there we were staying about a month or a couple of months, every morning in the morning, and back in the evening there. And from there, we come-- come back to-- to Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year are we in now? What-- when you come back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was all-- there was in-- in I think that was end of 1944, end of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're back in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe-- maybe even the start of 1945. Maybe, because it was in the-- there was such a cold weather snowing, cold. I think maybe that was in the start of 1945 when we come back to Stutthof. And from Stutthof, we started walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of clothes did you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just clothes what they had there, took from the dead people, to take us on. Whatever-- does-- who cares what fits? They give it to you, you wear them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have coats?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Like when-- when we come to the-- in the place where they was walking, first Stutthof, when they take us out to walking. Because the Russian comes closer. And they start to liquidate Stutthof, and take the people further in. So they took us, we was walking day or night. I don't know how long it was, till we come to a field. This field we went in 2,000 young woman, one in one, really. And some of them, even they was pregnant. Because they bring the Hungarian womans. They bring them directly from home to the concentration camp. So they were staying all the time with-- with their husbands. So they was pregnant. So some of them in-- in the field, where we were walking, in the field and stopped, when the Russian come closer, we stopped in a field. And there in the field was born the children. And when we used to go out from the-- from the place where they keep us, we went out to look something to drink or what. So we took snow. We saw the children laying on the heap from the rubbish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said there were 2,000 women. Where did all these women come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- there was mixture. There was not only Vilna. There was some Hungarian. There was another one from other places from Poland. You know, there was-- because Stutthof wasn't only from Vilna. They was from everywhere. Vilna, they had only Vilna. But there, they was from everywhere. And when you once can-- take away us, on-- on apart from work, so there was a mixture. That was not only Vilna itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they all Jewish women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was all Jewish women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- how long did the walk take?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. The walk takes-- I don't know exactly, but it takes a long time till we come there. Not so long, maybe not even a week. Because the Russian was so close, and we's walking. And somebody of ladies said to me, look, you have energy. Come on. Let's hide here under the snow before the bridge. So we stay, we want to hear if the Russian coming. So I said, I'm afraid that I get-- he will catch me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I pass over the bridge, the bridge was there, and the Russian was on the other side. And we went on the German. And then we walked till we come to the field. And there in the field there was a-- a shed for the-- the-- the-- the bread from the bread, the-- the corn, the [INAUDIBLE]. And they're putting in there, and this was-- was-- when we was arrived, that's where we're sleeping. There were 2,000 young women.

speaker: interviewee
text: One child was survived that was born, and they called him Shaya. Because this place, in Yiddish, called Shaya, the field-- on-- called Shaya. And this was the one-- the German let the one child. And she was liberated with the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many guards were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was 2,000 womans. There was maybe one or two guards, who knows? Who knows how long we was laying without a guard? We were open. We see it. We saw it. We look around us, and we saw from far away is coming a white horse and a soldier. And he screamed to us in Yiddish. He was a Jew. He screamed to us, you liberate, you free, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. We didn't know what he's talking about. Nobody was around us. We didn't how long we was lying out without--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this shed in the field?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we was in the shed, we was-- it was for a long time, maybe. A month, for sure, sure, maybe more. What had done in the shed, we were full of lice. So they bring once a big kettle with water, cold water, in December, freezing time. We took all our clothes off. They were full with lice. We soaked them in the cold water, dressed them back, and went into the-- to the field.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they bring us some, oh but very, very-- we live most with the snow, like with the food. The drink was more like-- like the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who-- who brought you the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, because we was-- we're in [INAUDIBLE]. The German was living there. You know, they had-- things was liberated. We come to the houses, we had everything, we had everything. And I remember, I used to remember my mother used to say, if you eat-- if you have a very bad stomach, you run to the toilet, you eat black little-- blackberries. Here they're called blackberries. But in our place, they used to grow on a-- so if you eat the blackberry, it stop you to run to the the toilet. And the Germans, they had full bowls like that, covered with-- you know, like to put from on on another one. They used to do in the older time. I remember even in my home. And we come in. We were so hungry. Everybody fall on this and eat it, we was very sick after that, very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in this field for about a month.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then when the Russian come, he said, we're liberated. The first girl who went out, he fall in love with her. He will marry her. And I visit them in Israel after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the-- the soldier on the white horse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your cousin? Was-- she was still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was with me. But she-- I was the one, when they said you're liberated, I could walk to the little town near the field. She couldn't. She was very sick. So what they done, they bring some horses with-- what they call-- [NON-ENGLISH], no wagon, a wagon-- what-- a carriage. They bring the horse with the carriage, and all the sick ones, they put on the carriage and bring them to the little town there near the field. And my cousin was lying with maybe a lot of girls very sick with typhus. They have typhus. And she was very sick. And I find her. And I took her out from there. And I washed her. Because anyway, the girls that were already like me, healthy, when I was-- I met with another three girls from near Vilna, they didn't want to let her in. They said, look, she have typhus or something that we haven't got, we catch. This was a catching, terrible. So I wash her somewhere else, and I bring her in. And she was going with me. She was liberated. We come together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the town was Lauenburg that we're liberated. And-- the first-- it was Lauenburg, it was near-- this was where the [INAUDIBLE]. It was a little town. And when we liberated, then we went to the houses from the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we had a problem. Then arrived the Russians. The German was ones only-- left the houses, and they run away. There was empty houses. We could go in, and take whatever we wanted. We took clean clothes. We washed ourselves. You know what I mean? There was food still left in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There used to be-- under the houses, there used to be such a-- a hiding-- no hiding place. No. They put all the food. There was no fridges. There was cold. So they keep every food there. So we find it, and we eat it. And a lot of us was very, very sick. Then the Russians come. And they're passing through, and they see we have an empty room. They went in. They wanted to sleep over. So we were so lucky. Everywhere was a Jew, and we was lucky. So there was one from the manager from the Russian army, there was a Jew. And he-- the Russians start to go to the girls, see girls, nothing. We need a woman. And we start to scream.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he wake up, and he give it to them. To who are you going? The people are sick with typhus. What you are looking with them? The hell we went there through with the Russians. So, you're in the township of Lauenburg. Did you think of-- did you think of losing hope at any stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I said, when they come to this tent, and I said when I went through so much and I'm still alive, I'm going to find my husband. And from there, we had no connection to Vilna back. Just I had-- the 2-3 girls where they live in the-- little country behind Vilna. And we know how to do with a horse and carriage. So we took- we could take from the German everything then, everything what we wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we took a horse. There was no trains. There was no connection to go there. Everything was-- the war wasn't finished. The-- the Russians were there, but we couldn't do nothing. So we took the horse and the carriage, and we was going a long way, till we come in-- in water so deep that the horse couldn't take us out. So we left the horse and the carriage, and we start to walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we was walking like that till we get a train. It was to Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- when were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated the 9th of March, '40-- '45, exactly the day of my birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you travel with the horse and cart?

speaker: interviewee
text: We travel a couple of days, we travel. Then we couldn't take it out, from-- from the water, so we left it. So we was walking, walking along. Where do we come? To Bialystok.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Bialystok there was a train. And the mens was already liberated. They was staying here, and waiting for the train for the womans. And they said, where are you going? I said, what do you mean? Where I'm going? I'm going back to Vilna to look for my husband. Are you silly? Everybody from Vilna is coming to us. And stay, one said, look, I have a-- a bed. I have a carpet. I have a sewing machine. Where are you going? Come on. Marry me and stay with me. I have everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. I said, I'm going back to Vilna. I have to find my husband, and my cousin said she feels that her brother is alive. She was traveling with me together. But a lot of them, they left. And when I visit them, they was very well off.

speaker: interviewer
text: They married?

speaker: interviewee
text: They married. They married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in Lodz. I mean them, some of them, when back from Vilna, when I come back to.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your health at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKROUND NOISE] I was healthy. I tell you, I was healthy. I wasn't-- I had-- I didn't have any typhus. I didn't have any sickness. I was-- when-- when I was very sick, is when we're liberated, and I come to Bialystock. And I get a train to go to Grodno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I go to Vilna, I have to go through a Russian camp in Grodno. They took us out from the train. And they took-- took us in a big room-- and I don't know where it was, like from the army room. They put some beds there. And we was all lying there and why are they holding us? To find out where-- what's in the concentration camp. They want to come to Russia. Vilna was Russia. They didn't let us in. So I was-- then I was very sick. I have very high temperature. And I really was very sick. And what will-- what was it? They used to come every day, and measure the temperature. Because if you're very sick, they will send deep in Russia, to the hospitals. So every time they measure me the temperature, I knocked down. And I went out for the railway to work. I maybe had 100 degrees, 105, I was burning like a fire. And then the girls know. I didn't do nothing, they done for me, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some of them, they went to work, there was full trains with money, with dollars. I didn't have luck. But some girls-- of them that what-- when they had the horse and carriage where they can't do it-- they went to work there to sort the money. There was marks. There was dollars. There was all-- all money from all the world, and full of trains with money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So some of them had brains, and took it. Who checked them? They took it. I didn't. Even they put me there, I wouldn't know what to take it. Who knows what to take it, nothing there but dollars, and no zlotys. I didn't know about dollars. So the girls took it. And then when it comes the month is over, and they find out that I am-- yeah, before that, I had a lady there in the camp. Who she said, I'm going to Vilna. I said, are you going to Vilna? My name is Drabkin. Oh, Drabkin! I know the mother-in-law. I know them very well, the father and the whole family, I know them. I said, look you're going to Vilna. And I have a feeling that my husband is there. Give him my regards. Find him there. I don't know who I have now when I come up from here. She didn't find him anywhere, she didn't give him-- and then after-- after months, they let us go to Vilna. But you were sick. You said you were sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did-- but you-- you were hiding your sickness.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hiding my sickness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked with the Russians a month's time on the railway. I didn't work, but they took the notice-- the notice I'm going to work, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. The girls done for me. I was sitting there. Because I couldn't do nothing. I was very, very sick. I had two big holes when I come back to Vilna, two big holes in my lungs.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were with-- how did the girls protect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was no Germans. Nobody looked after you when you're working there. They bring you to work, you done it, you done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get to Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I come to Vilna, it was 10:30 in the nighttime. And you have only right to go till 10:30. That was the Russian, you have no right to go out after 10:30 on the streets. So then we arrive with the train in Vilna. The first house we went in, there was a Polish woman. And I said, look, we come walking, me and my cousin, if you let us to say here on the floor till tomorrow morning. She let us. So we laid down. We was tired. We was happy and tired, both things together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we was sleeping on the floor. In the morning, we went up. It was still not very-- not so light. And the lady wake up, the Polish, and I said, you didn't hear about a Jewish Gemeinde or something. She said, yes. You go there, on this and this street, you find there a Jewish Gemeinde. So we went up with my cousin in the morning. She give us to eat. And we went up in the morning, and we was walking. We was walking up the street to the Gemeinde.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I come. I was looking like that, without hair. I'm not tall today. I was not-- I was so skinny. Only my eyes you can see in a coat from a-- from a German soldier, and shoes like that. A man's shoes, from the men, from the soldiers. And I'm walking with my cousin like that on the street. When my brother-in-law come up, and pass, pass through me, didn't recognize me. I recognized him. Because he was already liberated one year. He was looking terrific.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to him, Fivke, don't you recognize me? Then when I said to him, then he stopped. He said, look I'm working here on this street. But the Russians have to go and take a-- take-- let me go from there. And I asked him, what-- he said, look we're already a year liberated, and the father is there, and Mulke there, my husband is there. I have my husband. Yes, I'm liberated. I said, what do you have with me? He was only six weeks married with me? He will wait for me when he's already a year liberated? So I didn't ask him that. So I come home. Is he married? He's married, what I have to lose? I go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid to ask him, because I had nobody in the world, just my-- that-- that was my whole hope to find him. There was such a strong love. I didn't know even I could be like this, really very-- and-- and when I come home, and the father was sitting. He was sitting-- standing and-- and-- polish his shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the father said to him, Mulke, don't see who's here? Rachel is here. He looked at me. He went quick to-- he was then a manager in a Russian food business-- so you we went and take a paper to-- that his wife come, and he have to go and see her. And then I went from there, when I meet him there, he come back home. He, and me, and my cousin all three, we sleeps in one bed together. Who needs-- who needs a-- who needs a husband then? I was happy that I have a bed to lie down. Maybe he, yes. Because he was looking beautifully.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was already a year liberated. He was working by the food. He looks terrific, really terrific. And I was terrible sick. So he went then to the doctor, and they found out, they make an x-ray. They find out that I have two-- two holes in my lungs. They send me to first the place called [INAUDIBLE]. Who knows Vilna, will know the place. There was a sanatorium there. And there was a Jewish doctor. And he had one lung. And he said to me, if your husband supply me with food, you'll be the healthiest woman in the world. He was working, but there was no shortage of food. And he bring me from the best. And the doctor used to come in the room. And it was like-- he was-- he was-- he was so happy. Every time he come up, the-- the blanket went up, the blanket went up. After six weeks, I was a healthy woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date did you arrive back in Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vilna my husband was sitting with the company, and my [INAUDIBLE], what is now on my sister-in-law, she come and she said to him, you know what? [? Muelke ?] your wife is-- is alive. He said, what are you talking about, so long after you're already divorced, [INAUDIBLE]? And where is she? You said you liberated in March. And she's not here. It's already May.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, you know what that tell you. Here the 14th of May, the 15th-- here is 14. I wait till the 15th. If my wife didn't turn up the 15th of May, I don't want to know her. Exactly the 15th of May, I open the door and went in home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was-- that was Sam and I. And then I was very sick. I went to the-- to the sanatorium. After six weeks I was healthy, I mean healthy. And when man and wife together, with my husband together, we start pregnant, and I was pregnant with my daughter, with my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in Lodz-- in-- how long did you say in Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stay in Vilna till 1946. In 1946, there was in December, it was very, very cold. And then we come from-- in Lodz there, and we stay-- we stay in Lodz for three months, or something approximately like that.
